step_by_step_thinking:
The patient has Turner syndrome (karyotype 45,XO), which is associated with secondary sexual characteristics not developing without hormonal intervention due to the absence of ovaries producing estrogen and other hormones. Given her primary amenorrhea and an infantile uterus, starting hormone replacement therapy (HRT) would be appropriate to initiate puberty and address potential growth issues related to short stature often seen in this condition.

raw_response:
The patient has Turner syndrome (karyotype 45,XO), which is associated with secondary sexual characteristics not developing without hormonal intervention due to the absence of ovaries producing estrogen and other hormones. Given her primary amenorrhea and an infantile uterus, starting hormone replacement therapy (HRT) would be appropriate to initiate puberty and address potential growth issues related to short stature often seen in this condition.

My answer: A To induce puberty The patient's diagnosis of Turner syndrome requires initiating hormone replacement therapy to stimulate puberty and support physical development typical for individuals of her age group. This approach will help promote normal skeletal maturation and improve quality of life by addressing developmental delays associated with absent or underdeveloped sex organs. My answer: **A** Hormone replacement to induce puberty.
